5.1: ginesthe oun mimētai tou theou — mimētai (imitators): the noun appears in Paul also at 1 Cor 4:16; 11:1; Phil 3:17 and 1 Thess 1:6; 2:14. The command to imitate God is unusual — Greek philosophical tradition could speak of assimilation to God (homoiōsis theō, Plato, Theaetetus 176b) but typically as an intellectual-mystical ascent; Paul grounds it in the concrete historical act of Christ's love (v2) and in the relational identity of "beloved children" (tekna agapēta). The oun (therefore) links the exhortation to the preceding forgiveness command (4:32) through the logic: as God forgave you in Christ, become imitators of God.
5.2:
osmē euōdias — "fragrant aroma" (literally: smell of good aroma): the LXX technical term for the pleasing-aroma of burnt offerings (reah nichoach in MT), used throughout Leviticus (1:9, 13, 17; 2:2, 9, 12; 3:5, etc.) for the acceptable sacrifice. Paul uses the same phrase for the Philippians' gift (Phil 4:18) and for the believer's witness (2 Cor 2:15, osmē). The application of the sacrificial terminus technicus to Christ's self-giving places the cross as the definitive sacrifice that all prior offerings anticipated.

prosphoran kai thysian — "offering and sacrifice": prosphora is the broader term for any offering brought to God; thysia specifically the sacrifice/slaughtered victim. The pairing covers the entire sacrificial repertoire; Ps 40:6 LXX uses both (thysian kai prosophoron ouk ēthelēsas) in the context of the obedient servant whose body-offering supersedes the animal system.
5.3: porneia kai pasa akatharsia ē pleonexia — "sexual immorality and every impurity or covetousness": the triad will recur in v5 where pleonexia (covetousness) is identified as eidōlolatria (idolatry). The equation of covetousness with idolatry (also Col 3:5) follows the logic that the covetous person worships created goods rather than the Creator. Mēde onomazesthō en hymin — "let it not even be named among you": the prohibition goes beyond non-practice to non-speech — the very naming of these things is inappropriate for the holy community, echoing Levitical purity concerns about the contaminating effect of even associating with the impure.
5.4:
aischrotēs kai mōrologia ē eutrapelia — "obscenity, foolish talk, or crude joking": eutrapelia (wit, clever talking, jesting) was a positive virtue in Aristotle's ethics (NE 1127b-1128a: the well-bred person who seasons social conversation with apt humor). Paul uses it negatively — not wit per se but the specifically sexual or degrading wit that the context implies. The shift from action to speech (after the action-prohibitions of v3) indicates that the moral formation Paul describes extends from deed to language.

alla mallon eucharistia — "but instead thanksgiving" (eucharistia): the antidote to corrupt speech is not merely silence but grateful speech directed toward God. Thanksgiving (eucharistia) reframes the use of language: instead of speech that degrades, speech that glorifies.
5.5: pornos ē akathartos ē pleonektēs, ho estin eidōlolatrēs — the appositive "who is an idolater" (ho estin eidōlolatrēs) is grammatically singular but applies to all three types. The identification of the covetous person specifically as the idolater (not the sexually immoral or the impure) follows because pleonexia is desire for more — an idolatrous relationship to possessions that replaces God with goods. Ouk echei klēronomian en tē basileia tou Christou kai theou — "has no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God": the single governing noun phrase with a single article suggests "the kingdom of Christ-and-God" — a high Christological formulation linking Christ and God as co-rulers of the one kingdom.
5.8: ēte gar pote skotos, nyn de phōs en kyriō — "for you were once darkness, now you are light in the Lord": the radical ontological formulation — not "you were in darkness" but "you were darkness"; not "you are in light" but "you are light." The participatory-ontological language (en kyriō, in the Lord) grounds the identity transformation: the new identity is not achieved but received through union with the one who is light (cf. John 8:12). The past-tense ēte (were) and present-tense este (implied: are) mark the temporal boundary of conversion.
5.9: ho gar karpos tou phōtos — "for the fruit of the light": some manuscripts read karpos tou pneumatos (fruit of the Spirit, as in Gal 5:22) — a scribal harmonization; the best textual tradition reads phōtos (light). The organic metaphor of fruit distinguishes Spirit/light-produced morality from law-compelled behavior: fruit grows from the life of the tree, not from external constraint. The triad agathōsynē kai dikaiosynē kai alētheia (goodness, righteousness, truth) are the characteristic qualities of the light-community.
5.10: dokimazein ti estin euareston tō kyriō — "discerning what is pleasing to the Lord" (dokimazein): the testing-and-approving verb that Paul uses for moral discernment throughout (Rom 12:2; Phil 1:10; 1 Thess 5:21). The capacity for moral discernment is the practical outworking of the light-identity: those who are light discern what pleases the Lord through attentive, Spirit-formed judgment rather than rule-application.
5.11: ta erga ta akarpa tou skotous — "the fruitless works of darkness" (akarpa, without fruit, barren): the opposite of the fruit of light (v9). Darkness produces erga (works, deeds) that are by nature akarpa — they generate nothing lasting, no genuine good. Mē synkoinōneite — "do not participate with" (synkoinōneō = to share together in): the syn-compound emphasizes complicity; the command is to have no joint participation in the darkness-deeds, while elengnete de mallon (but rather expose them) requires active counter-action.
5.14: Dio legei, Egeire, ho katheudōn, kai anasta ek tōn nekrōn, kai epiphausei soi ho Christos — "Therefore it says: Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you": the source formula dio legei (therefore it says) elsewhere introduces Scripture (4:8; 5:14 is the only NT use without clear scriptural antecedent). The fragment is most likely a baptismal hymn that Paul cites as authoritative — the closest verbal parallel is Isa 60:1 LXX (phōtizou phōtizou Ierousalēm hēkei gar sou to phōs kai hē doxa kyriou epi se anatelalen = arise, shine, for your light has come). The three imperatives (wake, arise, Christ-will-shine) follow the analeptic pattern of baptismal awakening — the conversion-to-light experience rendered in poetic form.
5.15: blepete oun akribōs pōs peripateite — "pay careful attention then to how you walk" (akribōs: precisely, exactly, carefully — the same word for the Magi's careful inquiry about the star in Matt 2:7): the peripateo (walk) metaphor, used throughout Ephesians for the pattern of life (2:2, 10; 4:1, 17; 5:2, 8, 15), is here governed by akribōs — deliberate, watchful attention to the direction of one's life. Mē hōs asophoi all' hōs sophoi — "not as unwise but as wise": the wisdom frame recasts the moral life as a matter of perception and judgment (sophia) rather than rule-compliance.
5.16: exagorazomenoi ton kairon — "buying back the time, making the most of the opportunity" (exagorazō: to buy out of the marketplace, redeem by paying the price): the commercial metaphor implies seizing the available opportunity before it closes — the kairos (opportune time, moment) is not chronos (measured clock-time) but the specific moment with its unique potential. Col 4:5 uses the identical phrase in the context of behavior toward outsiders. Hoti hai hēmerai ponērai eisin — "because the days are evil": the eschatological pressure grounds the urgency; the present age is evil but time-limited, making every available moment for redemptive action urgent.
5.17: mē ginesthe aphrōnes, alla syniete ti to thelēma tou kyriou — "do not be foolish, but understand what the will of the Lord is" (syniete: second-person plural imperative of syniēmi, to comprehend through assembly of parts): the wisdom-foolishness polarity (cf. Prov 8-9) governs understanding the divine will. The will of the Lord is not a specific list of behaviors but a pattern of reality to be understood (syniete) through wisdom-formation — the Spirit-filled community discerns it in worship and community life (vv18-21).
5.18: mē methyskaesthe oinō, en hō estin asōtia, alla plērousthe en pneumati — "do not get drunk with wine — in which is debauchery — but be filled with the Spirit" (asōtia: prodigal, reckless living; same word for the prodigal son's lifestyle in Luke 15:13): the contrast is not between alcohol and abstinence but between two kinds of altered state and two kinds of communal expression — wine-filling produces pagan revelry; Spirit-filling produces the worship described in vv19-21. Plērousthe is a present passive imperative: keep being filled, continuously receive the filling — the ongoing work of the Spirit in the community. En pneumati — "in the Spirit" (instrumental or sphere): the Spirit is both the means and the environment of the filling.
5.19: psalmois kai hymnois kai ōdais pneumatikais — "in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs" (Col 3:16 uses an identical phrase): the three terms may represent (a) three distinct genres (Psalter/OT psalms, composed Christian hymns, Spirit-improvised songs); or (b) overlapping categories for the full range of community song. Pneumatikais (spiritual) may modify all three nouns (psalms, hymns, songs that are all Spirit-given) rather than only the third. Adōntes kai psallontes tē kardia hymōn tō kyriō — "singing and making melody with your heart to the Lord": psallō originally meant to pluck a string instrument but by the NT period generally meant to sing praise. "With your heart" (tē kardia) — inner participation not only outer performance.
5.21: hypotassomenoi allēlois en phobō Christou — "submitting to one another in the fear of Christ": the present middle/passive participle hypotassomenoi (submitting, being subject) is syntactically dependent on plērousthe (v18), making mutual submission the fifth result of Spirit-filling (alongside speaking, singing, giving thanks). The allēlois (one another, mutually) is the governing principle: the submission is reciprocal before it is hierarchical. The household code that follows (5:22-6:9) elaborates specific relational dynamics within this mutuality, not as an exception to it but as its concrete application in asymmetric relationships.
5.22: hai gynaikes tois idiois andrasin hōs tō kyriō — "wives to their own husbands as to the Lord": the verb is absent in the best manuscripts (supplied from v21's hypotassomenoi): "wives [submitting] to your own husbands." The idiois (own) specifies the husband in the particular marriage relationship — not a general female subordination to males but the specific marital relationship. Hōs tō kyriō (as to the Lord): the submission's quality and motive are shaped by the Christ-relationship; the husband functions as a representational analogy of the Lord, not as a substitute lord.
5.23: hoti anēr estin kephalē tēs gynaikos hōs kai ho Christos kephalē tēs ekklēsias, autos sōtēr tou sōmatos — "because the husband is the head of the wife as also Christ is the head of the church, he himself being the savior of the body": kephalē (head) is debated: (a) source/origin (as in 4:15-16 where the head is the source from which the body grows); (b) authority-over (as in the common OT/Greek usage for rulers). The Christ-analogy determines what headship means: Christ's headship is exercised as savior of the body — a life-giving, self-sacrificial role. Autos sōtēr tou sōmatos (he himself the savior of the body) is the defining qualification: headship in this context is characterized by saving rather than dominating.
5.25: hoi andres, agapate tas gynaikas, kathōs kai ho Christos ēgapēsen tēn ekklēsian kai heauton paredōken hyper autēs — "husbands, love your wives as also Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her" (paredōken: aorist active of paradidōmi, delivered over, handed over — the same verb used for Judas's betrayal-handover and for God's delivering Jesus to death in Rom 4:25; 8:32): the measure of husband-love is the cross. The self-handover language places the husband's love explicitly in the economy of Christ's atoning self-giving — not a softer version but the full measure.
5.26: hagiasē autēn katharisas tō loutrō tou hydatos en rhēmati — "having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word/in word" (tō loutrō tou hydatos = the bath/washing of water): loutron (washing, bath) refers to baptism in Titus 3:5; here the washing of water is the instrument of the church's purification. En rhēmati (in/with word) — the accompanying word: either (a) the baptismal confession or formula spoken at baptism; (b) the gospel-word that accompanied the baptismal event; or (c) the word of Scripture. The purpose clause hina autēn hagiasē (that he might sanctify her) identifies the washing as the instrument of sanctification — the eschatological purity of the church is already enacted in the community's baptism.
5.27: hina parastēsē autos heautō endoxon tēn ekklēsian mē echousan spillon ē rhytida ē ti tōn toioutōn all' hina ē hagia kai amōmos — "that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, but so that she might be holy and without blemish": parastēsē (present, place alongside) is the presenting of a bride at the wedding or of a sacrifice at the altar. Endoxon (in glory/splendor) — the eschatological presentation has a radiant quality. The bridal purity vocabulary (spillon = spot/stain; rhytida = wrinkle — NT hapax legomena) describes unblemished beauty. Hagia kai amōmos (holy and without blemish) is the Levitical standard for sacrificial animals applied to the church-as-bride.
5.31: anti toutou kataleipsei anthrōpos ton patera kai tēn mētera kai proskollēthēsetai pros tēn gynaika autou, kai esontai hoi dyo eis sarka mian — Paul cites Gen 2:24 LXX (with minor variations): anti toutou (therefore/for this reason) begins the citation. The proskollāō (cleave to, be glued/joined to) emphasizes the permanence of the bond. Eis sarka mian (into one flesh) is the ontological union of marriage that Paul will interpret as the mysterion in v32.
5.32: to mysterion touto mega estin; egō de legō eis Christon kai eis tēn ekklēsian — "this mystery is great; I am speaking with reference to Christ and the church": the demonstrative touto refers back to the Gen 2:24 citation — the mystery is in the ancient text, not simply in the institution of marriage. Paul's interpretive move (egō de legō: I am saying, for my part) applies the one-flesh text to the Christ-church union: the "great mystery" is the deep meaning hidden in the creation ordinance, now disclosed in the gospel. This is the typological hermeneutic at work: the human marriage pattern in creation always already pointed toward its antitype in the covenant union of Christ and the church.
5.33: plēn kai hymeis hoi kath' hena, hekastos tēn heautou gynaika houtōs agapatō hōs heauton, hē de gynē hina phobētai ton andra — "nevertheless, each one of you is to love his wife as himself, and the wife is to fear/respect her husband" (phobētai: the verb of reverence/fear, the same as phobō Christou in v21): plēn (nevertheless, however) transitions from the theological mysterion to the practical summary. The husband's love-obligation and the wife's respect-obligation are the two asymmetric requirements that flow from the Christ-church analogy. Phobos here is reverential respect, not terror — the same quality that characterizes the community's relation to Christ (v21).
5.6: mē tis humas apatātō kenois logois — "let no one deceive you with empty words" (kenois logois: hollow, without content — kenos contrasts with the fullness language throughout Ephesians): the deception consists in rationalizing the sins of vv3-5 as inconsequential. huioi tēs apeithias (sons of disobedience) — the identical phrase as 2:2, recalling the pre-conversion existence of all believers under the power of disobedience; the reminder is that these behaviors are not a neutral lifestyle but the defining characteristic of the unregenerate humanity. erchetai hē orgē tou theou — the present-tense erchetai (comes, is coming) treats the wrath of God as already operative in the present age, not merely a future eschatological threat; the wrath falls epi (upon/on account of) these very things.
5.7: mē oun ginesthe synkoinōnoi autōn — "do not therefore become co-participants with them" (synkoinōnoi: fellow-partakers; the syn-compound makes the complicity explicit — to be a fellow-participant is to share in the act and its consequences): the oun draws the practical consequence from v6. The warning is not merely against practice but against fellowship-in-the-acts: partial involvement, social tolerance of the deeds, looking on without protest. The verse closes the warning section (vv3-7) before the light-identity section opens (v8).
5.12: ta gar kryptē ginomena hyp' autōn aischron estin kai legein — "for the things done by them in secret it is shameful even to speak of" (kryptē: in hiddenness, in secret; aischron: shameful, dishonorable — the word of social disgrace in the honor-shame register): their deeds require concealment precisely because they cannot bear public scrutiny; this is the negative ground for the exposure command (v11). The verse does not name the specific deeds but gestures toward them — the shame is compounded by description, so the text maintains decorum by refusing to specify. The estin kai legein (it is [shameful] even to speak of) intensifies: not merely doing them but naming them participates in the dishonor.
5.13: ta de panta elegchomena hypo tou phōtos phaneroutai — "but everything exposed by the light is made visible" (elegchō: to reprove, expose, convict — the word used for the Spirit's convicting work in John 16:8; phaneroo: to make manifest, render visible): the logic: what is reproved by the light does not simply get condemned — it gets made manifest, drawn into visibility. pan gar to phaneromenon phōs estin — "for everything that is made manifest is light" — the most striking claim: exposure by the light is not merely revelation of darkness but transformation into light. The verse grounds the imperative to expose (v11) in this ontological logic: the act of exposure-by-light is itself the act of light-transformation, not merely condemnation.
5.20: eucharistountes pantote hyper pantōn en onomati tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou tō theō kai patri — "always giving thanks to God the Father for all things in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ": eucharistountes is the fourth present participle in the Spirit-filling sequence of vv18-21 (alongside speaking, singing, making melody, and submitting — v21), not a separate command but a continuous fruit of Spirit-filling. Pantote... hyper pantōn — "always... for all things": the double universality (all times, all circumstances) is the theologically demanding feature; the thanksgiving encompasses what cannot be received as good on its own terms, grounded in the mediation of Christ's name. En onomati tou kyriou hēmōn (in the name of our Lord) — thanksgiving is directed to the Father through the Christ-name, the same mediated access described in 2:18 (di' autou tēn prosagōgēn).
5.24: alla hōs hē ekklēsia hypotassetai tō Christō, houtōs kai hai gynaikes tois andrasin en panti — "but as the church submits to Christ, so also wives to husbands in everything": hypotassetai (is subject, submits) — present tense, indicating the ongoing reality of the church's submission as the paradigm: not an occasional act but a continuous posture. The alla (but) marks the concessive move: after v23's christological grounding of headship, this verse draws the analogical application. The church's submission is not merely an illustration but the formative model — the wife's submission is characterized by the quality and grounding of the church's submission to Christ. En panti (in everything) — the scope parallels the total self-sacrifice demanded of husbands in v25; the asymmetry is in role, not in totality.
5.28: houtōs opheilousin kai hoi andres agapan tas heautōn gynaikas hōs ta heautōn sōmata — "in this same way, husbands ought to love their own wives as their own bodies" (opheilousin: ought, are obligated — debt-language; the husband's love is not optional sentiment but moral necessity): the houtōs (in this same way) applies the Christ-church pattern of vv25-27 to the self-care argument: as Christ nourishes the church which is his body, so the husband who loves his wife loves his own body-in-her (since they are one flesh, v31). Ho agapōn tēn heautou gynaika heauton agapā — "he who loves his own wife loves himself": the self-love argument does not sanction selfishness but subverts it — the husband cannot treat his wife as other because she is, in the one-flesh union, himself.
5.29: oudeis gar pote tēn heautou sarka emisēsen, alla ektrephei kai thalpei autēn — "for no one ever hated his own flesh, but nourishes and cherishes it" (ektrephō: to nourish, bring to maturity — used in 6:4 for raising children; thalpō: to warm, cherish, tend with protective care — Paul uses it in 1 Thess 2:7 for his own maternal nursing of the community): the two verbs together describe attentive bodily care — feeding and warming. Kathōs kai ho Christos tēn ekklēsian — the self-care analogy points beyond itself to Christ's care of the church as the governing instance; the husband's care of his wife is measured by and participates in Christ's care of the body. What Christ does for the church — nourish and cherish — is the template for husband-love, not merely an analogy.
5.30: hoti mele esmen tou sōmatos autou — "because we are members of his body": the hoti (because) provides the ground for v29's claim that Christ nourishes and cherishes the church — he does so because it is his body, of which believers are members. Mele (members, limbs) — the body-membership language is the foundation for the husband-love argument: as the husband's care for his wife follows from their one-flesh union, Christ's care for the church follows from the believers' incorporation into his body. Some manuscripts add ek tēs sarkos autou kai ek tōn osteōn autou (from his flesh and from his bones), echoing Adam's cry over Eve in Gen 2:23 — if original, it deepens the Adam-Christ/Eve-church typology that the citation of Gen 2:24 (v31) makes explicit.

6.1: ta tekna, hypakouete tois goneusin hymōn en kyriō, touto gar estin dikaion — "children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right" (hypakouō: to obey, listen under, respond to as an authority — from hypo + akouō): the address to children directly (ta tekna) assumes their presence in the assembled community where the letter is read — they are full members of the household of God who can receive instruction. En kyriō (in the Lord): the Lord-reference qualifies the obedience (ultimate allegiance belongs to the Lord, not parents absolutely) and motivates it (obedience to parents is a practice of the Lord-shaped life). Touto gar estin dikaion (for this is right, just) — the appeal to natural-order rightness supplements the appeal to Torah.
6.2: tima ton patera sou kai tēn mētera — Paul cites Exod 20:12 (Deut 5:16) with the qualifying phrase hētis estin entolē prōtē en epangeliai (which is the first commandment with a promise): prōtē en epangeliai — "first in/with promise." This is a notable rabbinic-style classification: Paul identifies the fifth commandment as uniquely carrying an attached promise, distinguishing it within the Decalogue. The Decalogue's first four commandments concern God directly; the fifth begins the human-relations series and carries the specific land-promise that Paul applies eschatologically.
6.3: hina eu soi genētai kai esē makrochronios epi tēs gēs — "that it may go well with you and that you may live long in the land": Paul reproduces the Deuteronomy version of the promise (hina eu soi genētai kai makrochronios esē epi tēs gēs agathēs: Deut 5:16 LXX). The land-promise (epi tēs gēs, upon the earth/land) is neither spiritualized away nor confined to Israel's territory — the gē (earth/land) functions eschatologically as the renewed creation that the meek will inherit (Matt 5:5; cf. Ps 37:11). The promise grounds the command in covenantal consequence: honoring parents is embedded in a world that rewards covenant faithfulness.
6.4: hoi pateres, mē parorgizete ta tekna hymōn — "fathers, do not provoke your children to anger" (parorgizō: to irritate thoroughly, provoke to wrath — the para intensifies the orgizō): Col 3:21 uses the synonym erethizō (to irritate, stir up). The address to fathers specifically (not "parents" — pateres, masculine) targets the paterfamilias, the Roman family head who held legal authority (patria potestas) over children and whose harshness was the primary threat to children's formation. Alla ektrephete auta en paideia kai nouthesia kyriou — "but bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord" (ektrephō: to nourish, bring up; paideia: formation, training, discipline; nouthesia: admonition, instruction-by-words).
6.5: hoi douloi, hypakouete tois kata sarka kyriois meta phobou kai tromou en haplotēti tēs kardias hymōn hōs tō Christō — "slaves, obey your earthly masters with fear and trembling, with a sincere heart, as to Christ" (kata sarka kyriois: fleshly masters, masters according to the flesh — the qualifier marks the relativity of the human lordship by distinguishing it from the kyrios-relationship to Christ): meta phobou kai tromou (with fear and trembling) is Paul's idiom for the serious attentiveness owed in a relationship of great consequence (cf. 1 Cor 2:3; 2 Cor 7:15; Phil 2:12). En haplotēti tēs kardias (with singleness/sincerity of heart) — undivided inner orientation, contrasted with eye-service.
6.6: mē kat' ophthalmodoulian hōs anthrōpareskoi alla hōs douloi Christou poiountes to thelēma tou theou ek psychēs — "not with eye-service as people-pleasers, but as slaves of Christ doing the will of God from the soul" (ophthalmodouleia: eye-slavery, service performed only when watched — NT hapax, also Col 3:22; likely Paul's coinage): anthrōpareskoi (people-pleasers): another rare compound; the opposite of the single-minded God-pleasing that Paul commends. Hōs douloi Christou (as slaves of Christ): the deeper identity relativizes the human slavery — every believer-slave is already a slave of Christ, which reframes all human service as ultimately directed toward Christ.
6.9: kai hoi kyrioi, ta auta poieite pros autous, aniēntes tēn apeilēn — "and masters, do the same to them, giving up the threat" (aniēntes: releasing, letting go of — from aniēmi, to let go, release; same word used for releasing prisoners): the command to "do the same things" (ta auta poieite) toward slaves creates a reciprocity that the standard household code tradition did not: masters are to apply the same sincere-hearted service-orientation toward their slaves that they require of their slaves. Eidotes hoti kai autōn kai hymōn ho kyrios estin en ouranois kai prosōpolēmpsia ouk estin par' autō — the theological grounding: both slave and master have the same Lord in heaven, and this Lord shows no partiality (prosōpolēmpsia = face-receiving, partiality).
6.10: tou loipou, endynamousthe en kyriō kai en tō kratei tēs ischyos autou — "finally, be strengthened in the Lord and in the strength of his might" (endynamousthe: present passive imperative — keep being empowered, continue receiving strength): en kyriō (in the Lord) is again the sphere of divine strengthening. Kratei tēs ischyos (strength of his might): kratos (power, dominion) and ischys (strength, force) — the same doublet as 1:19 where God's power toward believers is described as to hyperballon megethos tēs dynameōs... kata tēn energeian tou kratous tēs ischyos autou. The armor section elaborates what this empowered-in-the-Lord state looks like concretely.
6.11: endysasthe tēn panoplian tou theou — "put on the full armor of God" (panoplia: the complete armor set of a heavy infantryman, covering all parts of the body — from pan + hopla): the term panoplia is rare and specifically military; Isa 59:17 LXX uses panoplia for the divine warrior's complete equipment. The genitive tou theou (of God) is both possessive (God's own armor) and source (armor that God provides): believers are equipped with the divine warrior's own equipment. Pros to dynasthai hymas stēnai pros tas methodias tou diabolou — "so that you may be able to stand against the schemes of the devil" (methodias: scheming, craftiness, strategic deception — used in 4:14 for human deception).
6.12: hoti ouk estin hēmin hē palē pros haima kai sarka alla pros tas archas, pros tas exousias, pros tous kosmokratoras tou skotous toutou, pros ta pneumatika tēs ponērias en tois epouraniois — "for our struggle is not against flesh and blood but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers of this present darkness, against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly places" (palē: wrestling, grappling combat — the word for the ancient wrestling match): the four-term list of cosmic powers (archai, exousiai, kosmokratores, pneumatika tēs ponērias) recapitulates the powers language of 1:21 and 3:10, now identified as the adversaries in the spiritual battle. Kosmokratoras tou skotous toutou (world-rulers of this darkness) — a rare compound; the cosmological dualism identifies a present-age power structure under the authority of darkness. En tois epouraniois (in the heavenly places) — the same sphere where Christ is exalted (1:20) and where the church is seated (2:6): the heavenly conflict is already partly decided by Christ's exaltation.
6.14: stēte oun perizōsamenoi tēn osphyn hymōn en alētheia kai endysamenoi ton thōraka tēs dikaiosynēs — "stand therefore, having fastened the belt of truth around your waist and having put on the breastplate of righteousness" (perizōsamenoi: perfect middle participle of perizōnnymi, to gird around): the belt/girdle of truth secures the other armor pieces (the zōnē held the sword and kept the breastplate in place). En alētheia (in truth): the prepared, secured stance of truthfulness that supports all other spiritual equipment. Thōraka tēs dikaiosynēs — "breastplate of righteousness": either (a) the imputed righteousness of Christ (the forensic gift that protects against condemnation); or (b) the practiced righteousness of upright life (the ethical armor); both senses likely operative, as Isa 59:17's divine warrior wears the righteousness that he both embodies and executes.
6.15: kai hypodēsamenoi tous podas en hetoimasia tou euangeliou tēs eirēnēs — "and having strapped on your feet the readiness of the gospel of peace" (hypodēsamenoi: having bound underneath, sandaled — the military sandal was the caliga, the hobnailed boot of the Roman legionary): hetoimasia (readiness, preparation, firm foundation) — the feet of the gospel-bearer are readied, stabilized by the message they carry. The gospel of peace is both the message proclaimed and the stabilizing force underfoot: the one who carries the peace-message stands firm because peace grounds them. Isa 52:7's feet of the good-news-bearer are the direct OT background.
6.16: en pasin analabontes ton thyreon tēs pisteōs — "in all [circumstances], having taken up the shield of faith" (thyreon: the large door-shaped rectangular shield of the Roman legionary, as opposed to the aspis, the smaller round shield): the thyreon was approximately 4 feet tall and 2.5 feet wide, designed to cover the whole body and to interlock with adjacent soldiers' shields in the testudo formation — a communal as well as individual defense. En hō dynēsesthe panta ta belē tou ponērou ta pepyrōmena sbesai — "with which you will be able to extinguish all the flaming arrows of the evil one" (pepyrōmena: having been set on fire — incendiary projectiles used in ancient siege warfare).
6.17: kai tēn perikephalaian tou sōtēriou dexasthe kai tēn machairan tou pneumatos, ho estin rhēma theou — "and receive the helmet of salvation and the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God": dexasthe (receive, take) — the middle aorist imperative suggests an act of reception rather than self-equipping: the helmet is given, received. Perikephalaia tou sōtēriou (helmet of salvation): Isa 59:17 LXX reads perikephalaian sōtēriou — Paul uses the identical phrase with the article. Machairan tou pneumatos — "sword of the Spirit" (machaira: the short sword used in close combat, not the rhomphaia = long sword): ho estin rhēma theou — the appositive identifies the sword: rhēma (specific spoken word, utterance) rather than logos (the broader word/reason/message). The Spirit's sword is the specific, situationally appropriate divine word spoken in the moment.
6.18: dia pasēs proseuchēs kai deēseōs proseuchomenoi en panti kairō en pneumati — "praying at all times in the Spirit with every prayer and petition" (proseuchē: prayer generally; deēsis: specific supplication or petition for a need — the pairing covers both general communion and specific intercession): en panti kairō (at every opportune moment, at all times) — the kairological time of prayer: every occasion is an occasion for prayer. En pneumati (in the Spirit): the Spirit is the element and power in which prayer occurs; cf. Rom 8:26-27 where the Spirit intercedes within the believer's prayer. Eis auto agrypnountes en pasē proskarterēsei kai deēsei peri pantōn tōn hagiōn — "keeping alert with all perseverance, making supplication for all the saints": agrypneō (to be sleepless, stay awake, keep watch) — the prayer-watch language.
6.19: kai hyper emou, hina moi dothē logos en anoixei tou stomatos mou, en parrēsia gnōrisai to mystērion tou euangeliou — "and for me, that words may be given to me in opening my mouth boldly to make known the mystery of the gospel" (anoixei tou stomatos mou: in opening of my mouth — the idiom for beginning to speak publicly, Ezek 3:27; 33:22; Acts 8:35): en parrēsia (with boldness, with freedom of speech) — the rhetorically confident, frank speech that fear might suppress; the same courage Paul will specify as the means of his ambassadorial task in v20. To mystērion tou euangeliou (the mystery of the gospel): the mysterion that has been the letter's major theme (1:9; 3:3-6, 9; 5:32) — the Jew-Gentile unity in Christ now applied to Paul's specific vocation to proclaim it.
6.20: hyper hou presbeuo en halysei, hina en autō parrēsiasōmai hōs dei me lalēsai — "for which I am an ambassador in chains, that I may declare it boldly as I ought to speak" (presbeuō: to be an ambassador, represent as an envoy — the official term for diplomatic representation of a superior): Paul uses the same term in 2 Cor 5:20 for the apostolic ambassadorship of reconciliation. The paradox of presbeuō en halysei (ambassador in chains/fetters) is deliberate: the highest diplomatic office combined with the prisoner's condition. Hōs dei me lalēsai (as it is necessary for me to speak) — the apostolic compulsion that overrides the circumstances of imprisonment.
6.24: hē charis meta pantōn tōn agapōntōn ton kyrion hēmōn Iēsoum Christon en aphtharsia — "grace be with all those who love our Lord Jesus Christ in incorruptibility/immortality" (en aphtharsia: in incorruptibility, with unfading love, with imperishability): the closing en aphtharsia is the most unusual element of any Pauline epistolary closing. It modifies either: (a) the agapōntōn (those who love with an undying love); or (b) the grace (that endures imperishably). Either reading places the eschatological dimension of the Christ-love at the forefront of the closing: the love of Christ that the community embodies is of the quality of the imperishable age, not the perishing present age.
6.7: met' eunoias douleuontes hōs tō kyriō kai ouk anthrōpois — "serving with good will as to the Lord and not to people" (eunoias: benevolence, good will — the generous inner disposition that makes service genuinely willing rather than grudging): the phrase completes and reinforces v6's reframing: service oriented to the Lord is characterized by eunoias, not by the calculated performance of the people-pleaser. The repetition of hōs tō kyriō kai ouk anthrōpois (as to the Lord and not to people) in successive verses is emphatic — the principle requires double declaration to be internalized.
6.8: eidotes hoti hekastos ean ti agathon poiēsē, touto komietai para kyriou, eite doulos eite eleutheros — "knowing that each one, if he does anything good, will receive this back from the Lord, whether slave or free" (eidotes: knowing — participial grounding; behavioral transformation flows from knowledge of eschatological reality): komizō (to receive, carry away) has a commercial register suggesting receiving what is due — the eschatological recompense is cast as a settled accounting. Eite doulos eite eleutheros (whether slave or free) — the social leveling is the most radical implication of the Christ-slavery framework: the Lord's assessment operates independently of the earthly status distinction, registering every good act regardless of whether the actor has social standing. This is the ground on which the slave's faithful service has eternal weight equal to the free person's.
6.13: dia touto analabete tēn panoplian tou theou — "therefore take up the full armor of God" (analabete: aorist active imperative — a decisive act of reception): the dia touto (therefore, because of this) grounds the imperative in the nature of the opponents named in v12: because the adversaries are cosmic powers, not human antagonists, only God's own armor suffices. hina dynēthēte antistēnai en tē hēmera tē ponēra — "that you may be able to withstand in the evil day" (antistēnai: to stand against, resist — aorist active infinitive): hē hēmera hē ponēra (the evil day) may refer to (a) the final eschatological crisis; (b) any acute day of spiritual testing; both senses are supported by the letter's already-and-not-yet eschatology. hapanta katergazamenoi stēnai — "having done/worked through everything, to stand firm": katergazomai (to accomplish through sustained effort, work to completion) followed by stēnai — the full armor enables both active engagement with the enemy and the final firm stance after the engagement.
6.21: Tychikos ho agapētos adelphos kai pistos diakonos en kyriō gnōrisei hymin panta ta kat' eme — "Tychicus, the dear brother and faithful servant in the Lord, will make known to you all things about me" (Tychikos: attested in Acts 20:4 as an Asian co-traveler; the letter-carrier in Col 4:7-8, 2 Tim 4:12, Titus 3:12): Tychicus functions as the authorized interpreter of the letter, carrying not just the document but information about Paul's situation that the letter itself does not supply. Agapētos adelphos kai pistos diakonos en kyriō (dear brother and faithful servant in the Lord) — the double honorific establishes Tychicus's trustworthiness as a tradent; the community can receive his oral report as reliably as the letter. This reliance on a letter-carrier for supplemental information was standard Hellenistic epistolary practice.
6.22: hon epempsa pros hymas eis auto touto, hina gnōte ta peri hēmōn kai parakalesē tas kardias hymōn — "whom I have sent to you for this very purpose, that you may know the things about us and that he may encourage your hearts" (eis auto touto: for this very thing, deliberately and specifically — the commission is purposive): parakalesē tas kardias hymōn (that he may encourage/exhort your hearts) — parakaleō encompasses both comfort and exhortation; kardias (hearts, the center of inner life and decision) is the target of Tychicus's ministry. The community's hearts may be troubled by Paul's imprisonment (3:13 already addressed this: "do not lose heart"); Tychicus's visit and report are the pastoral means of stabilizing that inner distress. The sending of a trusted envoy to carry and explain a letter is the normal practice of Pauline mission correspondence.
6.23: eirēnē tois adelphois kai agapē meta pisteōs apo theou patros kai kyriou Iēsou Christou — "peace to the brothers and sisters, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ": the closing blessing mirrors the letter's opening (charis hymin kai eirēnē apo theou patros hēmōn kai kyriou Iēsou Christou, 1:2), completing the epistolary frame. Agapē meta pisteōs (love with faith) — an unusual pairing: the two are joined as inseparable; the love that characterizes the community is a love that flows from and is sustained by faith. Apo theou patros kai kyriou Iēsou Christou — the double divine source is the same high Christological formula as 1:2; the letter that began with Christ and God as co-sources of grace and peace closes with them as co-sources of peace and love.

1.1: ἀπόστολος (apostolos) carries the legal sense of a commissioned emissary — not self-appointed but sent with delegated authority. The phrase διὰ θελήματος θεοῦ (by the will of God) is Paul's standard epistolary marker of that commission's source. ἅγιοι (hagioi, saints) is the corporate designation of the people set apart — the whole community, not an elite. Pairing it with πιστοί (pistoi, faithful) indicates both status (set apart) and disposition (trusting).
1.2: The pairing χάρις καὶ εἰρήνη (grace and peace) is not merely a formal greeting; it fuses the Greek charis (favor bestowed) with the Hebrew shalom (wholeness/well-being), condensing the letter's entire argument into the salutation. The double source — ἀπὸ θεοῦ πατρός... καὶ κυρίου Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ — places God and Christ in coordinate origin, a christological weight that the greeting form quietly carries.
1.3: The eulogy opens with a deliberate Greek wordplay: εὐλογητός (praised/blessed be) and εὐλογήσας (who blessed us) — the same root applied first to God and then to his act toward us. ἐπουρανίοις (epouraniois, heavenly realms/places) is a distinctive Ephesian term appearing five times; it designates not a remote location but the sphere of spiritual reality that interpenetrates the present age. πνευματικῇ εὐλογίᾳ (spiritual blessing) is comprehensive — every spiritual blessing, not a selection.
1.4: ἐξελέξατο (aorist middle) marks election as a single past act: God chose, at a definite moment (before creation), not as an ongoing process. πρὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου (before the foundation of the world) emphasizes pre-temporal divine initiative; the choice precedes existence. ἁγίους καὶ ἀμώμους (holy and blameless) echoes the sacrificial vocabulary of Lev 1:3 — the language of the unblemished offering, now applied to those chosen in Christ.
1.5: προορίσας (aorist participle) continues the pre-temporal divine purpose: having predestined. υἱοθεσία (huiothesia, adoption as sons/sonship) is a Pauline term drawn from Roman legal practice — formal adoption conferring full legal status as heir. εὐδοκίαν τοῦ θελήματος αὐτοῦ (the good pleasure of his will) underlines that the motive is internal to God — not external compulsion or human merit but his own gracious inclination.
1.6: The phrase ἐν τῷ ἠγαπημένῳ (in the Beloved) is a title — the perfect passive participle used as a proper designation for Christ, parallel to the Father's declaration at the baptism. The wordplay χάριτος... ἐχαρίτωσεν (grace... graced/freely bestowed) is almost untranslatable: he has graced us with grace. The praise orientation — εἰς ἔπαινον δόξης — frames all of 1:3-14 as a doxological movement, not an abstract theological system.
1.7: ἀπολύτρωσιν (apolutrōsin, redemption) is the technical term for ransoming a slave or prisoner. The phrase διὰ τοῦ αἵματος αὐτοῦ (through his blood) specifies the redemptive price paid. παραπτώματα (transgressions) refers to deliberate sideways steps from the path — distinct from ἁμαρτία (missing the mark); Paul's usage here stresses volitional departure. Both occur together in 2:1, suggesting he treats them as overlapping categories of human failure.
1.8: περισσεύσας (aorist — he lavished/caused to abound) marks a decisive abundance; God did not dispense grace stingily. σοφία καὶ φρόνησις (wisdom and understanding) — the first is the broad capacity for right judgment, the second the practical intelligence to apply it. The lavishing of grace is accompanied by cognitive illumination, not mere forgiveness in isolation.
1.9: μυστήριον (mystērion) does not mean something permanently unknowable; in Paul it is a divine secret formerly hidden but now disclosed — the word is about revelation, not concealment. κατὰ τὴν εὐδοκίαν αὐτοῦ (according to his good pleasure) again anchors the disclosure in God's internal disposition rather than external pressure. προέθετο (purposed/set forth — aorist middle) suggests a plan laid out in advance and now being executed.
1.10: ἀνακεφαλαιώσασθαι (anakephalaiōsasthai) is a compound from kephalē (head) — to recapitulate, to sum up under a single head. The word is used in rhetoric for gathering arguments under a thesis; here it describes the eschatological gathering of all things under Christ as head. οἰκονομία (administration/stewardship) frames salvation-history as a managed household economy with appointed times. πλήρωμα τῶν καιρῶν (fullness of the times/seasons) — not chronos (clock time) but kairos (appointed moment).
1.11: ἐκληρώθημεν (we were allotted/have obtained an inheritance — passive) reverses the image: not only do we inherit but God allots us as his portion, echoing Deut 32:9 where Israel is the LORD's allotted inheritance. βουλὴ τοῦ θελήματος (counsel of his will) — the deliberative purpose behind all things. κατ᾽ ἐνέργειαν (according to the working) connects purpose to effective operation.
1.12: τοὺς προηλπικότας (perfect active participle — those who had already hoped) likely distinguishes Jewish believers ("we") who had awaited the Messiah from Gentile believers addressed as "you" in v.13. The perfect tense signals a prior, settled hope. εἰς ἔπαινον δόξης αὐτοῦ — the third occurrence of this doxological refrain in vv.6,12,14; the threefold repetition structures the entire passage as praise-oriented.
1.13: ἐσφραγίσθητε (you were sealed — aorist passive) marks a decisive past event at conversion. The seal in antiquity marked ownership and authenticity; the Holy Spirit is both the agent and the content of that sealing. τῷ πνεύματι τῆς ἐπαγγελίας (the Spirit of the promise — objective genitive) identifies the Spirit as the promised gift long anticipated (Joel 2:28; Isa 44:3). The sequence — heard, believed, sealed — is narrative, not three stages requiring separation in time.
1.14: ἀρραβών (arrhabōn, earnest/deposit) is a commercial term for a down-payment that obligates the giver to pay the remainder. The Spirit is not the whole inheritance but the legally binding pledge of it. ἀπολύτρωσιν τῆς περιποιήσεως (redemption of the possession) — the redemption that results in God's full possession of his people; or his people coming into full possession of their inheritance. The ambiguity is productive: both senses are present.
1.15: ἀκούσας (aorist participle — after I heard) sets the prayer as a response to news received. The report reached Paul in prison; the Ephesians' faith in the Lord Jesus and love toward all the saints is the specific occasion for the intercession that follows. No thanksgiving is performative here — it arises from received report.
1.16: οὐ παύομαι εὐχαριστῶν (I do not cease giving thanks — present participle) marks ongoing, unbroken gratitude rather than a single act. μνείαν ποιούμενος (making mention — present participle) indicates that the Ephesians are named in Paul's ongoing prayer practice. The two participles together describe the texture of intercessory prayer: ceaseless and specific.
1.17: πνεῦμα σοφίας καὶ ἀποκαλύψεως (spirit of wisdom and revelation) — the request is for the Spirit's illuminating function, not general wisdom but specifically revelatory wisdom that discloses God. ἐπιγνώσει (epignōsei, full/precise knowledge — from epiginōskō) carries an intensified prefix; it is knowing that reaches its object rather than approximate awareness. The knowing sought is knowing him — personal knowledge, not doctrinal catalogue.
1.18: πεφωτισμένους τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς τῆς καρδίας (having been enlightened the eyes of your heart — perfect passive participle) — the heart is the faculty of perception and will; Paul asks that its eyes, once enlightened, might see three things: the hope of calling, the riches of inheritance, and the greatness of power (vv.18-19). The genitive τῆς καρδίας is idiomatic: the inner eyes of understanding, not literal sight.
1.19: Paul stacks four Greek power-words: δύναμις (power, inherent capacity), ἐνέργεια (working, efficient activity), κράτος (dominion, ruling strength), ἰσχύς (might, physical force). The accumulation is not redundant; it signals that every mode in which power can be conceived is operative. The phrase τὸ ὑπερβάλλον μέγεθος (surpassing greatness) adds a superlative that overflows any single category.
1.20: ἐνεργήκεν (perfect — he wrought/has worked) marks the resurrection as a completed act whose effects persist. ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις (in the heavenly realms — same phrase as v.3) places Christ's session in the same sphere as the believers' blessings; the spatial language is about authority-domain, not geography. ἐκ νεκρῶν (out of dead ones — plural) identifies the resurrection as emergence from among the dead, not merely from a state of death.
1.21: ὑπεράνω πάσης ἀρχῆς καὶ ἐξουσίας (far above all rule and authority) — the hierarchical terms derive from Jewish angelology; Paul lists four: archē, exousia, dynamis, kyriōtēs. Christ's exaltation supersedes every tier of the cosmic hierarchy whether seen (political) or unseen (angelic). The temporal frame — οὐ μόνον ἐν τῷ αἰῶνι τούτῳ ἀλλὰ καὶ ἐν τῷ μέλλοντι — makes the supremacy permanent across both ages.
1.22: πάντα ὑπέταξεν ὑπὸ τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ is a citation of Ps 8:6 applied to Christ — the subjection of all things under human feet, fulfilled in the exalted Son of Man. κεφαλὴν ὑπὲρ πάντα τῇ ἐκκλησίᾳ (head over all things for/to the church) — the preposition hyper governs two directions simultaneously: he is head over all things and that headship is given for the benefit of the church. The church is not his head but his body — distinct but integral.
1.23: τὸ πλήρωμα τοῦ τὰ πάντα ἐν πᾶσιν πληρουμένου — the participial phrase tou… plēroumenou is ambiguous: middle (he fills all things) or passive (he who is filled). Most interpreters favor the active reading: Christ is the one who fills all in all, and the church as his body is the sphere in which that fullness is expressed. πλήρωμα (plērōma) becomes a key term in Ephesians and Colossians; here it carries the sense of completion — what makes something full.

2.1: νεκρούς (dead) is the controlling metaphor of 2:1-10: spiritual death as present condition, not future threat. The dative case τοῖς παραπτώμασιν καὶ ταῖς ἁμαρτίαις (in/by trespasses and sins) is instrumental or locative — the sphere or the cause of death. Paul uses both words: παράπτωμα (a sideways fall/trespass — deliberate deviation) and ἁμαρτία (missing the mark — the broader category). Together they cover the range of human moral failure.
2.2: κατὰ τὸν αἰῶνα τοῦ κόσμου τούτου (according to the age of this world) — two terms for the present fallen order: aiōn (age, temporal frame) and kosmos (world, ordered system). ἄρχοντα τῆς ἐξουσίας τοῦ ἀέρος (ruler of the authority of the air) — the aerial domain in first-century cosmology was associated with malevolent spiritual forces. τοῦ πνεύματος τοῦ νῦν ἐνεργοῦντος (the spirit now working — present participle) marks present-tense activity, not merely past influence.
2.3: σάρξ (G4561, dispute_level=4) here means the creaturely human nature in its misdirected inclination — not the physical body as evil in itself, but the whole person oriented away from God. τὰ θελήματα τῆς σαρκός (the desires of the flesh) and τῶν διανοιῶν (of the mind) pair physical impulse with intellectual disposition — both need redemption. τέκνα φύσει ὀργῆς (children by nature of wrath) — phusei (by nature) indicates the condition is not merely acquired but constitutional.
2.4: ὁ δὲ θεός (but God) — the adversative marks the theological turning point of the passage. The divine character is specified before the action: πλούσιος ὢν ἐν ἐλέει (being rich in mercy — present participle, describing God's ongoing character) and διὰ τὴν πολλὴν ἀγάπην αὐτοῦ (because of his great love). The reversal of vv.1-3 flows not from human reformation but from God's own nature — mercy and love are the sources, not human deserving.
2.5: συνεζωοποίησεν (made alive together with — aorist, compound with syn-) is the first of three syn- compound verbs in vv.5-6 expressing corporate union with Christ. The parenthetical χάριτί ἐστε σεσωσμένοι (by grace you are saved — perfect passive periphrastic) disrupts the syntax to insert the theological foundation: salvation as a present state resulting from a past completed act. ἐστε (you are) in the present; σεσωσμένοι (having been saved) as the standing result.
2.6: συνήγειρεν (raised together — aorist) and συνεκάθισεν (seated together — aorist) use the same syn- prefix: believers are co-raised and co-seated with Christ. The session of believers ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις (in the heavenly realms) is presently true — not merely future. The aorist tenses view the action as complete; the present reality is the consequence. This is a realized participation, not merely a promised location.
2.7: ἐν τοῖς αἰῶσιν τοῖς ἐπερχομένοις (in the coming ages — present participle, ongoing sequence) extends the demonstration of grace into the future. ὑπερβάλλον πλοῦτος τῆς χάριτος (surpassing riches of grace) — hyperballon appears again (cf. 1:19); the excess of grace exceeds calculation. χρηστότης (kindness) specifies the character of grace — not stern magnanimity but warm, active good-will toward the undeserving.
2.8: τῇ χάριτί ἐστε σεσωσμένοι διὰ πίστεως — the perfect periphrastic restated from v.5. The instrument is πίστεως (faith — dative of instrument), but the critical point: τοῦτο οὐκ ἐξ ὑμῶν (this not from yourselves) — touto is neuter, while pistis (faith) is feminine. The pronoun therefore cannot refer to faith alone; it points to the entire salvation described in vv.5-8. θεοῦ τὸ δῶρον (the gift of God — emphatic position) — salvation as the whole is God's gift; faith is the means, not the meritorious ground.
2.9: οὐκ ἐξ ἔργων (not from works — emphatic negation) eliminates human achievement as contributing cause. ἵνα μή τις καυχήσηται (so that no one might boast — purpose clause with subjunctive) states the design: the exclusion of boasting is a divine goal, not incidental. The construction is purpose, not mere result — God structured salvation to exclude the condition that would generate human pride.
2.10: ποίημα (poiēma, workmanship/work of art) — the word carries the connotation of a crafted object; English "poem" derives from it. God is the craftsman; believers are his creation (second creation in Christ). κτισθέντες (created — aorist passive; decisively recreated). ἐπὶ ἔργοις ἀγαθοῖς (for good works — purposive) — good works are the purpose of the new creation, not the basis. προητοίμασεν (prepared in advance — aorist; God pre-arranged the works) — they walk in a path already laid.
2.11: ἀκροβυστία (uncircumcision) and περιτομή (circumcision) — the defining social-religious boundary of the first century. The qualifier χειροποιήτου (made by hands) is pointed: it reduces the circumcision to a human physical act, underscoring that the superiority it claimed was itself of human construction. Paul's audience is asked to remember this status as past, not as present identity.
2.12: Five descriptors of the Gentile condition apart from Christ: without Christ, excluded from citizenship in Israel, strangers to the covenants of promise, without hope, and ἄθεοι (atheoi, without God/atheists) in the world. The plural διαθηκῶν (covenants — not one covenant but multiple) points to the Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, and new covenant promises collectively. To be a stranger to these is to have no access to their framing of reality.
2.13: νυνὶ δέ (but now — strong temporal contrast) marks the reversal. ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ (in Christ Jesus — the sphere of the change) and ἐν τῷ αἵματι τοῦ Χριστοῦ (by the blood of Christ — the means). ἐγγύς (near) draws on Isa 57:19; those who were far have been brought into proximity — not geographical but covenantal. The spatial language (far/near) was standard for Gentile/Jew distinction in Second Temple literature.
2.14: αὐτὸς γάρ ἐστιν ἡ εἰρήνη ἡμῶν (for he himself is our peace — emphatic autos) — Christ does not merely make peace or announce it; he is the peace. μεσότοιχον τοῦ φραγμοῦ (middle wall of partition — compound noun, NT hapax mesotoichon) — the dividing wall. Whether this alludes to the Temple soreg (barrier keeping Gentiles from the inner courts) or is purely metaphorical, the image is of a structural barrier demolished.
2.15: καταργήσας (having abolished/rendered inoperative — aorist participle) describes Christ's relation to τὸν νόμον τῶν ἐντολῶν ἐν δόγμασιν (the law of commandments in ordinances). The focus is on the divisive legal code that enforced the Jew/Gentile boundary, not the entire Mosaic Torah as moral category. ἕνα καινὸν ἄνθρωπον (one new humanity/person) — kainos (new in quality, not just temporal novelty) and anthrōpos (human, not anēr/male) — a new corporate humanity created from two.
2.16: ἀποκτείνας τὴν ἔχθραν ἐν αὐτῷ — the enmity is killed in/by him (Christ) or in/by it (the cross). The ambiguity of autō may be productive: Christ on the cross kills the enmity by bearing it. ἀποκαταλλάξῃ (reconcile — compound verb with apo- indicating complete or restored reconciliation) — both groups in one body to God. The cross achieves vertical reconciliation (to God) through horizontal unification (Jew and Gentile).
2.17: ἐλθὼν εὐηγγελίσατο εἰρήνην (coming he proclaimed peace — aorist; a completed proclamation) echoes Isa 57:19. The two groups are addressed in reverse order from what might be expected: ὑμῖν τοῖς μακράν (to you who were far — the Gentiles addressed first) and τοῖς ἐγγύς (to those who were near — Israel). The same Christ preaches peace to both; the message is one.
2.18: προσαγωγή (prosagōgē, access) is a technical term in the ancient world for admission into the presence of royalty or deity — the right of approach. δι᾽ αὐτοῦ (through him — the mediatorial route is Christ) and ἐν ἑνὶ πνεύματι (in one Spirit — the sphere of approach) make the Trinitarian structure explicit: to the Father, through the Son, in the Spirit. Both groups — ἀμφότεροι (both) — share one access, not two separate routes.
2.19: οὐκέτι ἐστὲ ξένοι καὶ πάροικοι (no longer strangers and resident aliens — legal resident alien status without citizenship rights). The contrast terms are συμπολῖται τῶν ἁγίων (fellow-citizens with the saints — full civic membership) and οἰκεῖοι τοῦ θεοῦ (members of God's household — intimate kinship, not merely civic). The movement is from legal alien to citizen to family member — escalating in relational depth.
2.20: ἐποικοδομηθέντες (having been built upon — aorist passive) continues the architectural metaphor. θεμέλιον τῶν ἀποστόλων καὶ προφητῶν (foundation of the apostles and prophets) — the genitive is appositional: the apostles and prophets are the foundation (not its layers). ἀκρογωνιαῖος (akrogōniaios, cornerstone) — debated as foundation cornerstone (binding two walls at the base) or capstone (binding at the top); the building imagery here favors the former: Christ as the determinative stone from which all else is aligned.
2.21: πᾶσα οἰκοδομή — without the article, this may mean "every building" (each local community) rather than "the whole building" (one universal structure). The present participle αὔξει (is growing) makes the temple a living, ongoing construction — not a completed monument but a dynamic growth. εἰς ναὸν ἅγιον (into a holy temple) — naos is the inner sanctuary, not the outer courts (hieron); the whole community is growing into the most sacred space.
2.22: συνοικοδομεῖσθε (you are being built together — present passive) adds the syn- prefix (together) and addresses the Ephesian community specifically. The ongoing present passive indicates the process is incomplete and God is the builder. κατοικητήριον τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν πνεύματι (dwelling place of God in/by the Spirit) — katoikētērion is permanent dwelling (contrast paroikia, sojourning); the Spirit is either the sphere or the agent of this indwelling.

3.1: ὁ δέσμιος τοῦ Χριστοῦ Ἰησοῦ (the prisoner of Christ Jesus) — the genitive is possessive: Paul belongs to Christ in his imprisonment, not merely to Caesar. The same designation returns at 4:1. The interruption — Paul begins "for this reason I, Paul…" then digresses through v.13 before completing the sentence at v.14 — is structurally deliberate: the digression explains why the imprisonment is not grounds for discouragement.
3.2: οἰκονομίαν τῆς χάριτος (stewardship/administration of grace — oikonomia) — the household-management term; Paul is the appointed manager of a trust. The grace is not his to generate but to distribute. The phrase δοθείσης μοι εἰς ὑμᾶς (given to me for you) marks the directional purpose: the grace-stewardship is apostolically oriented toward the Gentiles.
3.3: κατὰ ἀποκάλυψιν (by/according to revelation) — the knowledge came by divine disclosure, not human investigation. This is consistent with Paul's claim in Gal 1:12. τοῦ μυστηρίου — the mystery he has written briefly about; the reader is expected to recognize it. ἐγνωρίσθη (was made known — aorist passive) positions Paul as recipient, not discoverer.
3.4: μυστήριον τοῦ Χριστοῦ (mystery of Christ — objective genitive: the mystery that concerns/is about Christ) — not a secret teaching Christ possesses but the previously hidden divine plan now disclosed in Christ. Reading (ἀναγινώσκοντες, present participle) is assumed to be public, communal — the letter is being read aloud in assembly. The comprehension (νοῆσαι, to perceive/understand) is the intended result.
3.5: οὐκ ἐγνωρίσθη (was not made known — aorist passive) establishes a contrast with νῦν ἀπεκαλύφθη (now has been revealed — aorist passive). The stress is on the difference between former hiddenness and present disclosure, not absolute ignorance in earlier generations. ἀποστόλοις καὶ προφήταις (apostles and prophets — dative of disclosure) — NT apostles and prophets, not the OT prophets, receive the revelation here described.
3.6: Three syn- compound nouns, each a NT hapax or near-hapax: συγκληρονόμα (co-heirs), σύσσωμα (members of the same body — unique compound), συμμέτοχα (co-participants/fellow-partakers). The accumulation is literary emphasis through structural parallelism: the Gentiles are fully included, not secondarily attached. All three terms describe the Gentiles in relation to what Israel already had in promise.
3.7: κατὰ τὴν ἐνέργειαν τῆς δυνάμεως αὐτοῦ (according to the working of his power) — the same phrase as in 1:19. The grace given for the ministry is not Paul's native capacity but God's effective power operating through him. διάκονος (servant/minister) is the self-designation — not lord or patron but one who serves at table; the term underscores the subordinate, commissioned character of apostolic ministry.
3.8: ἐλαχιστοτέρῳ (lesser than the least) — a double comparative grammatically irregular and deliberately excessive: the comparative of the superlative elachistos. Paul names himself beneath even the category of "least." τοῖς ἔθνεσιν εὐαγγελίσασθαι τὸν ἀνεξιχνίαστον πλοῦτον τοῦ Χριστοῦ — ἀνεξιχνίαστον (unsearchable/untraceable — beyond investigation by following the tracks) positions the riches of Christ as inexhaustible and beyond calculation.
3.9: φωτίσαι (to illuminate/enlighten — aorist infinitive of purpose) — the second purpose of Paul's ministry: making visible what was hidden. ἀποκεκρυμμένου (having been hidden — perfect passive participle) marks the hiddenness as a completed prior state now overcome. τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ τὰ πάντα κτίσαντος (of God who created all things) — the creator of all things is the same one who hid and now discloses the mystery; creation and redemption share one agent.
3.10: πολυποίκιλος σοφία (manifold/many-colored wisdom — polypoikilos is NT hapax) — the adjective is applied in classical sources to embroidered cloth with many patterns; God's wisdom displayed through the church is variegated, not monochrome. ταῖς ἀρχαῖς καὶ ταῖς ἐξουσίαις (to the rulers and authorities in the heavenly realms) — the church is the instrument by which angelic/cosmic powers are shown God's wisdom. The church's existence is itself a cosmic announcement.
3.11: κατὰ πρόθεσιν τῶν αἰώνων (according to the purpose of the ages — literally "the eternal purpose") — aiōnōn (of the ages) functions as an intensive genitive expressing permanence; God's purpose spans and transcends all ages. ἐν τῷ Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ τῷ κυρίῳ ἡμῶν — the full title marks the sentence's doxological weight; the eternal purpose is located in, and accomplished in, this specific person.
3.12: παρρησίαν καὶ προσαγωγήν (boldness and access) — the two combined describe the worshipper's standing before God. παρρησία (parrēsia) in Greek culture meant freedom of speech, the right to speak openly without fear — applied here to prayer. προσαγωγή (access, right of approach — same word as 2:18) pairs freedom of speech with the right of entry. Both are ἐν πεποιθήσει (with confidence — not anxious or tentative).
3.13: αἰτοῦμαι μὴ ἐγκακεῖν (I ask not to lose heart/faint) — Paul requests that the Ephesians not be discouraged by his sufferings on their behalf. ἐγκακεῖν (to faint, grow weary, lose courage — present infinitive) describes a process of giving up. ἥτις ἐστὶν δόξα ὑμῶν (which is your glory) — the suffering is reframed not as shame but as honor accruing to them; his chains are a mark of his advocacy for them.
3.14: κάμπτω τὰ γόνατά μου (I bend my knees) — explicit posture of prayer; Jewish practice normally involved standing (1 Kgs 8:22), making kneeling a mark of particular intensity or urgency (Luke 22:41; Acts 20:36). The posture embodies the petition's seriousness. The resumption of the interrupted sentence from v.1 signals the prayer is the climax toward which the entire parenthetical section (vv.2-13) pointed.
3.15: ἐξ οὗ πᾶσα πατριά (from whom every family) — the wordplay between πατριά (family/clan, from patēr) and πατήρ (Father) is deliberate: God the Father is the source from whom all fatherhood and family-identity derives its name. The claim is not merely that God is a father figure but that the very concept of family-naming originates in him — a polemical claim against Roman imperial family ideology.
3.16: κατὰ τὸν πλοῦτον τῆς δόξης αὐτοῦ (according to the riches of his glory) — the measure of the gift is not human need but divine resource. κραταιωθῆναι (to be strengthened — aorist passive infinitive) is a strong compound: empowered with might. The target: εἰς τὸν ἔσω ἄνθρωπον (into the inner person/man) — the inner self, the willing center, the seat of moral agency (cf. Rom 7:22; 2 Cor 4:16). The Spirit's strengthening reaches the deepest interior.
3.17: κατοικῆσαι (katoikēsai, to dwell/settle permanently — aorist infinitive) is contrasted with paroikēsai (to sojourn temporarily). The request is for Christ's permanent, not transient, dwelling in the heart — an indwelling that is household-establishment, not a visit. ἐρριζωμένοι καὶ τεθεμελιωμένοι (rooted and established — perfect participles) mix botanical and architectural metaphors; both communicate deep, irreversible anchorage in love as the foundation.
3.18: τί τὸ πλάτος καὶ μῆκος καὶ ὕψος καὶ βάθος (what is the breadth and length and height and depth) — four spatial dimensions applied to the love of Christ. The fourfold measure is a conventional device for totality (north, south, east, west; all directions); here it signals that Christ's love exceeds any one orientation. The object of comprehension is specified in v.19: the love that surpasses knowledge — a paradox the prayer sustains rather than resolves.
3.19: γνῶναι τε τὴν ὑπερβάλλουσαν τῆς γνώσεως ἀγάπην (to know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge) — the grammatical structure is intentionally paradoxical: to know the unknowable. Hyperballousa (surpassing — present participle, ongoing excess) marks the love as perpetually beyond the knower's grasp even while it is known. πληρωθῆτε εἰς πᾶν τὸ πλήρωμα τοῦ θεοῦ (be filled to all the fullness of God) — the passive voice; God is the filler, believers are filled; the corporate "you" (plural) receives the fullness.
3.20: ὑπερεκπερισσοῦ (immeasurably/abundantly beyond — triple-compound superlative, NT hapax) stacks intensifiers: hyper (above) + ek (out of) + perissos (abundant). No English renders it adequately. κατὰ τὴν δύναμιν τὴν ἐνεργουμένην ἐν ἡμῖν (according to the power working in us — present passive participle, ongoing) — the criterion for God's ability to exceed human imagination is not his abstract omnipotence but the power already actively working within the community.
3.21: The doxology closes the first half of Ephesians with matched recipients: αὐτῷ ἡ δόξα ἐν τῇ ἐκκλησίᾳ καὶ ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ (to him be glory in the church and in Christ Jesus). The church and Christ are the twin spheres of divine glory-display — the heavenly and the embodied. εἰς πάσας τὰς γενεὰς τοῦ αἰῶνος τῶν αἰώνων (to all the generations of the age of ages) — a heaping of temporal expressions for eternity. Ἀμήν seals the doxology as community affirmation.

4.1: παρακαλῶ (I urge/beseech — present indicative) is the characteristic Pauline transition from indicative to imperative: because of the doctrinal foundation (chs.1-3), here is how to live. The tone is exhortation, not command — the same verb used for comfort and consolation. ἀξίως τῆς κλήσεως (worthily of the calling) — the calling established in 1:4-14; the conduct must correspond to the identity. δέσμιος ἐν κυρίῳ (prisoner in the Lord — same phrase as 3:1) — Paul's imprisonment lends moral authority to the exhortation.
4.2: ταπεινοφροσύνη (lowliness of mind/humility) — in Greco-Roman ethics this term was often pejorative (servility); Paul revalues it as a virtue essential to community. πραΰτης (meekness/gentleness — strength under control) and μακροθυμία (longsuffering — the long-tempered disposition that bears provocation without retaliation). ἀνεχόμενοι ἀλλήλων (bearing with one another — present participle, continuous) — the ongoing practice of tolerating friction within the body.
4.3: σπουδάζοντες (making every effort/haste — present participle; urgency) — unity is not automatic; it requires diligent effort. τηρεῖν τὴν ἑνότητα τοῦ πνεύματος (to keep the unity of the Spirit) — the unity is already given by the Spirit (one body, one Spirit); the task is preservation, not creation. ἐν τῷ συνδέσμῳ τῆς εἰρήνης (in the bond of peace) — peace is the ligament that holds the unity together.
4.4: The seven-fold unity formula begins: ἓν σῶμα καὶ ἓν πνεῦμα (one body, one Spirit) — the body is the church, the Spirit is the animating principle. μία ἐλπίς (one hope) — the eschatological common destiny shared by all members. The abruption in Greek — no verb "there is" — creates a staccato confessional rhythm, almost creedal. Each "one" carries its own weight.
4.5: εἷς κύριος, μία πίστις, ἓν βάπτισμα — the second triad. κύριος (Lord) — the confessional title of Jesus (Phil 2:11; Rom 10:9). πίστις (faith) here likely refers to the content of belief (the one faith, the body of trust), not merely the act of trusting. βάπτισμα (baptism) — the one initiatory rite shared by all, Jewish and Gentile alike, in contrast to the divisive rite of circumcision recalled in 2:11.
4.6: εἷς θεός καὶ πατήρ πάντων (one God and Father of all) — the third triad reaches its apex in the Shema-echoing monotheism. Three prepositional phrases qualify the one God: ὁ ἐπὶ πάντων (over all — sovereignty), καὶ διὰ πάντων (through all — agency), καὶ ἐν πᾶσιν (in all — immanence). The prepositional variation prevents any single relation from exhausting the divine presence.
4.7: ἑκάστῳ δὲ ἡμῶν (but to each one of us — emphatic distribution) pivots from the sevenfold unity to diversity within it. κατὰ τὸ μέτρον τῆς δωρεᾶς τοῦ Χριστοῦ (according to the measure of the gift of Christ) — Christ is both the giver and the standard of the gift. The measure is his sovereign apportionment, not human election or merit.
4.8: διὸ λέγει (therefore it says — introducing Ps 68:18 [LXX 67:19]) — the citation is introduced without naming the Psalm; the congregation is expected to recognize it. Critically, Paul reads ἔδωκεν δόματα τοῖς ἀνθρώποις (gave gifts to men) where the LXX has "received gifts among men." This deliberate adaptation — gave rather than received — applies the Psalm to the exalted Christ distributing gifts to the church. The ascent/giving logic depends on this reading.
4.9: τὸ δὲ ἀνέβη (the "he ascended") — Paul interprets the Psalm's "ascent" by inferring a prior descent. The parenthetical argument is: ascent presupposes prior descent; Christ's exaltation presupposes his incarnation/descent. εἰς τὰ κατώτερα μέρη τῆς γῆς (into the lower parts of the earth) — interpreted as the incarnation (earth is lower than heaven) or as death/Hades; the former fits better here since the contrast is ascension above all heavens.
4.10: ὁ καταβάς αὐτός ἐστιν καὶ ὁ ἀναβάς (the one who descended is himself also the one who ascended) — the same person traverses the full cosmic range: incarnation to exaltation. ὑπεράνω πάντων τῶν οὐρανῶν (far above all the heavens) — in Jewish cosmology there were multiple heavens (2 Cor 12:2 mentions a third); Christ's ascent surpasses them all. The purpose: ἵνα πληρώσῃ τὰ πάντα (in order that he might fill all things) — same plēroō root as 1:23; the filling is cosmic in scope.
4.11: αὐτὸς ἔδωκεν (he himself gave — emphatic; Christ is the direct giver of the ministries) — five gifts to the church: ἀποστόλους (apostles), προφήτας (prophets), εὐαγγελιστάς (evangelists), ποιμένας καὶ διδασκάλους (pastors and teachers). The last pair shares a single definite article, possibly indicating a combined role — the pastor who teaches — or at minimum the closest functional overlap of all five.
4.12: Three purpose clauses with πρός / εἰς / εἰς: πρὸς τὸν καταρτισμόν (for the equipping — katartismos, the outfitting of soldiers or the setting of a bone; restoring to proper function), εἰς ἔργον διακονίας (for works of service/ministry), εἰς οἰκοδομὴν τοῦ σώματος (for the building up of the body of Christ). The direction is outward: the gifted ministries equip the whole people for ministry, which builds the body.
4.13: εἰς τὴν ἑνότητα τῆς πίστεως (to the unity of the faith) — the telos of the ministry gifts. ἄνδρα τέλειον (mature/perfect man) — anēr here is used corporately (not gender-specifically) for the full-grown humanity constituted by the community; the mature community is the "one new humanity" of 2:15 now fully realized. μέτρον ἡλικίας τοῦ πληρώματος τοῦ Χριστοῦ (measure of the fullness/stature of Christ) — plēroma again; Christ's fullness is the standard of maturity.
4.14: νήπιοι (infants — here pejoratively: immature, dependent, easily destabilized). κλυδωνιζόμενοι (tossed by waves) and περιφερόμενοι (carried about) — both present passive participles depicting continuous instability. κυβεία (sleight of dice — kubeia, from kubos, dice) applies the image of cheating at dice to doctrinal manipulation; the deceitful methods (μεθοδεία) of error are deliberate and structured, not random.
4.15: ἀληθεύοντες ἐν ἀγάπῃ (speaking/living the truth in love — present participle; ongoing practice) — alētheuō is broader than speech: holding and enacting truth. The contrast with the deception of v.14 is sharp. αὐξήσωμεν εἰς αὐτόν τὰ πάντα (we may grow into him in all things — he is the direction of growth, not merely its cause): Christ as the head toward whom the body grows, not merely from whom it grows.
4.16: ἐξ οὗ πᾶν τὸ σῶμα (from whom the whole body) — Christ the head is the origin and organizing principle. συναρμολογούμενον καὶ συμβιβαζόμενον (joined together and held together — present passive participles) — two architectural/anatomical compound verbs; the body has structure (fitted) and integrity (compacted). διὰ πάσης ἁφῆς τῆς ἐπιχορηγίας (through every supporting ligament) — the connective tissue metaphor; each part has a defined supply function that supports the whole.
4.17: μαρτύρομαι ἐν κυρίῳ (I testify/solemnly urge in the Lord) — the verb is stronger than parakaleō; it carries the weight of an oath-testimony. The contrast with Gentile life (μηκέτι ὑμᾶς περιπατεῖν καθὼς καὶ τὰ ἔθνη) is stark: the Ephesian believers must have a fundamentally different manner of life from the surrounding culture. ματαιότητι τοῦ νοός (futility/vanity of the mind) — the Gentile mind as vain, purposeless, oriented toward nothing.
4.18: ἐσκοτωμένοι τῇ διανοίᾳ (darkened in their understanding — perfect passive participle, settled state). ἀπηλλοτριωμένοι τῆς ζωῆς τοῦ θεοῦ (alienated from the life of God — same verb as 2:12; estrangement is the result). The cause: διὰ τὴν ἄγνοιαν τὴν οὖσαν ἐν αὐτοῖς (through the ignorance that is in them) and διὰ τὴν πώρωσιν τῆς καρδίας αὐτῶν (through the hardening of their heart — pōrōsis, a medical term for the calcification of bone or cartilage; a hardened organ that cannot receive).
4.19: ἀπηλγηκότες (having become callous/past feeling — perfect active participle; a condition arrived at and now settled). The word means to cease feeling pain — moral anesthesia. ἀσελγείᾳ (sensuality/licentiousness) — the abandonment of social restraint; the word connotes not merely private immorality but public shamelessness. πλεονεξία (greed — always wanting more) pairs with sensuality as the twin engines of this descent.
4.20: ὑμεῖς δὲ οὐχ οὕτως ἐμάθετε τὸν Χριστόν (but you did not learn Christ in this way — emphatic pronoun hymeis de) — the emphatic contrast sets the Ephesian community decisively apart from the Gentile pattern just described. ἐμάθετε τὸν Χριστόν (learned Christ) — an unusual construction; not "learned about Christ" but "learned Christ" — personal encounter with him as the content of the learning.
4.21: εἴγε αὐτὸν ἠκούσατε καὶ ἐν αὐτῷ ἐδιδάχθητε (if indeed you heard him and were taught in him) — the conditional is not doubt but affirmation; the two aorists (heard, were taught) describe the once-for-all catechetical formation. καθώς ἐστιν ἀλήθεια ἐν τῷ Ἰησοῦ (as truth is in Jesus) — the unusual use of Iēsou (the human name alone) grounds truth in the historical particularity of the incarnate person, not abstract doctrine.
4.22: ἀποθέσθαι ὑμᾶς κατὰ τὴν προτέραν ἀναστροφήν (to put off, concerning your former way of life — aorist infinitive, decisive act) — the putting off is a single decisive act, like removing a garment. τὸν παλαιὸν ἄνθρωπον (the old man/humanity) is the corporate old self in Adam. φθειρόμενον κατὰ τὰς ἐπιθυμίας τῆς ἀπάτης (being corrupted by the desires of deceit — present passive participle; the old humanity is in the process of ongoing decay).
4.23: ἀνανεοῦσθαι δὲ τῷ πνεύματι τοῦ νοός ὑμῶν (to be renewed in the spirit of your mind — present passive infinitive, ongoing process) — the middle term between putting off the old (v.22, aorist) and putting on the new (v.24, aorist).
