Renewal is continuous. πνεύματι τοῦ νοός (spirit of the mind) is a distinctive genitive — the animating principle or disposition of the mind; it may echo the pneuma/nous distinction in contemporary philosophy, here united rather than opposed.
4.24: ἐνδύσασθαι τὸν καινὸν ἄνθρωπον (to put on the new humanity — aorist, decisive action). καινός (new in quality, not merely recent — contrast neos) — the new humanity is qualitatively different, not just temporally new. κατὰ θεὸν κτισθέντα (created according to God — cf. 2:10; the new self is a divine creation, not human self-improvement). ἐν δικαιοσύνῃ καὶ ὁσιότητι τῆς ἀληθείας (in righteousness and holiness of truth) — dikaiosynē (righteous character toward others) and hosiotēs (holiness/piety toward God) as the twin dimensions of the new life.
4.25: ἀποθέμενοι τὸ ψεῦδος (having put off falsehood — aorist participle; the decisive removal that precedes what follows) and λαλεῖτε ἀλήθειαν (speak truth — present imperative, ongoing). The specific ground: ὅτι ἐσμὲν ἀλλήλων μέλη (because we are members of one another). Honesty is not merely ethical but ecclesiological — lying to a fellow member is a form of self-harm to the body.
4.26: ὀργίζεσθε καὶ μὴ ἁμαρτάνετε (be angry and do not sin) — a quotation of Ps 4:4 LXX. The construction is best read as concessive imperative: "if you are angry, do not sin" or "even if anger comes, do not let it lead to sin." Paul is not commanding anger but acknowledging its reality and limiting its damage. ὁ ἥλιος μὴ ἐπιδυέτω (let not the sun go down — prohibitive imperative) sets an urgency: anger resolved daily, not nursed into settled hostility.
4.27: μηδὲ δίδοτε τόπον τῷ διαβόλῳ (and give no place/foothold to the devil — present imperative prohibition) — topos (place) can be a spatial location, a legal opportunity, or a beachhead. Unresolved anger creates an opening (topos) that becomes an exploitable position. The connection between anger management and spiritual warfare is not incidental; Paul treats them as directly linked.
4.28: ὁ κλέπτων μηκέτι κλεπτέτω (the one who steals, let him steal no more — present participle and present imperative; the thief is characterized by his ongoing habit, and the command ends it). κοπιάτω ἐργαζόμενος τὸ ἀγαθόν (let him labor, working the good — present participle emphasizing the ongoing character of legitimate work). The purpose of honest labor: ἵνα ἔχῃ μεταδιδόναι (in order that he might have something to share) — work is oriented not toward accumulation but redistribution.
4.29: λόγος σαπρός (rotten/corrupt word — sapros is used of rotten fruit and fish) — speech that is decayed, worthless. The alternative: εἴ τις ἀγαθὸς πρὸς οἰκοδομήν (whatever is good for building up — oikodomē, same word as the body-building in vv.12,16) — speech is either demolition or construction. κατὰ τὴν χρείαν (according to the need) — the criterion is the hearer's specific situation, not the speaker's self-expression.
4.30: μὴ λυπεῖτε τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον (do not grieve the Holy Spirit — present imperative prohibition; stop or do not start grieving) — the Spirit is a person who can be grieved; the prohibition implies relational damage. The ground clause: ἐν ᾧ ἐσφραγίσθητε (in whom you were sealed — aorist passive, same verb as 1:13) — the sealing is the basis for the appeal; to grieve the Spirit is to damage the relationship that guarantees the inheritance.
4.31: Six terms for the corrosive interior and its expression: πικρία (bitterness — the root; the settled inner disposition of resentment), θυμός (passion/rage — sudden explosion of anger), ὀργή (anger — settled, burning anger), κραυγή (outcry/clamor — the vocal expression), βλασφημία (slander/abusive speech), and κακία (malice — the comprehensive term for wickedness). The list moves from interior attitude through verbal expression to general disposition.
4.32: χαριζόμενοι ἑαυτοῖς (forgiving one another — present participle; ongoing practice; charizomai from charis, grace) — forgiveness is literally grace-giving. The model: καθὼς καὶ ὁ θεὸς ἐν Χριστῷ ἐχαρίσατο ὑμῖν (just as God in Christ forgave you — aorist; the divine forgiveness is the completed event that establishes the pattern and the power for human forgiveness). The ethical imperative is grounded in the indicative of divine action.
